course syllabus
is professional elective 4 generative ai systems
instructor: jessie a. melendres 1st semester, a.y. 20262027 units: 3 schedule: tth 1:002:30 pm
1. course description
this course introduces students to the principles and applications of generative ai systems. topics include transformer architecture, large language models (llms), prompt engineering, retrieval-augmented generation (rag), vector databases, fine-tuning techniques such as lora and qlora, and llmops deployment practices

as lora and qlora, and llmops deployment practices. students will design and build a working generative ai application as a capstone mini-project that progresses through four checkpoints aligned with the four exam periods.
2. course intended learning outcomes (cilos)
by the end of this course, students should be able to:
cilo 1: build a full end-to-end data pipeline including text cleaning, tokenization, embedding generation, and dimensionality reduction.
cilo 2: design and implement a scalable

eduction.
cilo 2: design and implement a scalable rag architecture using a vector database and an orchestration framework such as langchain or llamaindex.
cilo 3: apply open-source foundation models and demonstrate understanding of peft, lora, and qlora fine-tuning techniques within memory constraints.
cilo 4: deploy a secure, containerized application using docker with a ci/cd-aware deployment workflow.
3. course topics by week
4. grading system
5. project checkpoints
6. course policies
attenda

5. project checkpoints
6. course policies
attendance: students are allowed a maximum of 3 absences. more than 3 absences may result in a grade of 5.0 or dropped status depending on the stage of the semester.
late submissions: a deduction of 10% per day late will be applied to all performance tasks. no submission will be accepted after 3 days past the deadline.
academic integrity: all code submissions must be the original work of the student or group. use of ai-generated code is permitted but mu

roup. use of ai-generated code is permitted but must be declared and explained. plagiarism results in automatic failure of the task.
laboratory requirements: students must bring their own laptop with python 3.10 installed. google colab is an acceptable alternative.
communication: official announcements will be posted on the course lms. students are expected to check the platform at least once daily.
7. required references
1. vaswani, a. et al. (2017). attention is all you need. neurips.
2. lewis

017). attention is all you need. neurips.
2. lewis, p. et al. (2020). retrieval-augmented generation for knowledge-intensive nlp tasks. neurips.
3. hu, e. et al. (2022). lora: low-rank adaptation of large language models. iclr.
4. langchain documentation.
5. huggingface documentation.
6. chromadb documentation.

course syllabus
business analytics
instructor: ba instructor name 1st semester, a.y. 20262027 units: 3 schedule: mwf 9:0010:00 am
1. course description
business analytics is a course that equips students with the skills to collect, process, analyze, and communicate data-driven insights for business decision-making. topics include descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive analytics, data quality management, statistical modeling, data visualization, and the use of modern analytics tools such as py

, and the use of modern analytics tools such as python, excel, and power bi. students will complete a series of performance tasks using real-world or simulated business datasets.
2. learning outcomes
lo 1: apply data quality management principles to identify and resolve issues in raw datasets.
lo 2: use descriptive statistics and visualization tools to summarize and present data effectively.
lo 3: build predictive models using regression and classification techniques.
lo 4: interpret analytics r

sification techniques.
lo 4: interpret analytics results and formulate data-driven business recommendations.
3. weekly topics
4. grading system
5. course policies
attendance: a maximum of 3 absences is allowed. more than 3 absences will result in a failing grade or dropped status.
late work: performance tasks submitted after the deadline will receive a 10% deduction per day. tasks submitted more than 3 days late will not be accepted.
tools required: microsoft excel (office 365), python 3.x with

ed: microsoft excel (office 365), python 3.x with pandas and matplotlib, power bi desktop (free version).
data privacy: any personal data used in exercises must be anonymized. students must not share datasets externally.
recitation: active participation in discussions and recitations is part of the grade. students should come prepared with the assigned readings.
6. required textbooks and references
1. shmueli, g. et al. (2023). data mining for business analytics. wiley.
2. few, s. (2012). show m

siness analytics. wiley.
2. few, s. (2012). show me the numbers: designing tables and graphs to enlighten. analytics press.
3. mckinney, w. (2022). python for data analysis. oreilly media.
4. microsoft power bi documentation.

course syllabus: information management 1st semester, a.y.
course information
instructor: im instructor name units: 3 schedule: mwf 10:0011:00 am room: it lab 2
1. course description
information management covers the principles and practices of managing organizational information
resources. topics include database systems, data modeling, information architecture, records
management, knowledge management, information security, and enterprise information systems.
students will analyze real-world i

mation systems.
students will analyze real-world information management problems and propose solutions using
standard frameworks and tools.
2. learning outcomes
lo 1: design and implement relational database schemas using normalization techniques.
lo 2: apply information architecture principles to organize and structure enterprise data.
lo 3: evaluate information security risks and recommend appropriate controls.
lo 4: use sql and database tools to retrieve and manipulate organizational data.
3.

to retrieve and manipulate organizational data.
3. weekly schedule
week topic assessment
1 introduction to information management; data vs informatqiouni zv s1 knowledge
2 database concepts; relational model; entity-relationshipl daiba g1rams
3 normalization; 1nf, 2nf, 3nf; functional dependencieslab 2
4 sql fundamentals; select, insert, update, deletpet 1
5 advanced sql; joins, subqueries, aggregate functions lab 3
6 information architecture; taxonomies and metadata quiz 2
7 records and documen

xonomies and metadata quiz 2
7 records and document management systems lab 4
8 midterm examination midterm exam
9 knowledge management systems; communities of practicpet 2
10 information security fundamentals; cia triad quiz 3
11 access control; authentication and authorization lab 5
12 enterprise information systems; erp and crm pt 3
13 data governance and compliance; gdpr, data privacy alcetcture
14 cloud-based information systems lab 6
15 case study: information management failure analysis gr

study: information management failure analysis group report
16 final examination final exam
4. grading system
component weight
quizzes 20%
laboratory exercises 20%
performance tasks 20%
midterm exam 20%
final exam 20%
5. course policies
attendance: students must maintain at least 80% attendance. absences beyond the allowable limit will
result in a grade of dropped.
laboratory: students are expected to save and back up all lab outputs. lost files due to negligence will
not be grounds for re-subm

due to negligence will
not be grounds for re-submission.
academic honesty: sharing of exam answers, plagiarism, or copying of laboratory outputs is a violation
of academic integrity and will be dealt with accordingly.
6. references
1. laudon, k. laudon, j. (2022). management information systems. pearson.
2. hoffer, j. et al. (2019). modern database management. pearson.
3. iso/iec 27001:2022 information security management standard.

course syllabus: systems analysis and design 1st semester, a.y.
course information
instructor: sad instructor name units: 3 schedule: tth 3:004:30 pm room: it lab 3
1. course description
systems analysis and design (sad) provides students with a structured approach to designing and
developing information systems. the course covers the systems development life cycle (sdlc),
requirements gathering, process modeling using data flow diagrams (dfds) and use case diagrams,
database design, prototyping

nd use case diagrams,
database design, prototyping, and system implementation. students will work in teams to analyze a
real-world problem and produce a complete system design proposal.
2. learning outcomes
lo 1: apply the sdlc methodology to plan and manage a software development project.
lo 2: elicit and document system requirements through interviews, surveys, and observation.
lo 3: create process models using dfds, flowcharts, and uml diagrams.
lo 4: design a functional prototype of an infor

s.
lo 4: design a functional prototype of an information system based on user requirements.
3. weekly schedule
week topic assessment
1 introduction to systems analysis; sdlc overview quiz 1
2 agile vs waterfall; project management basics discussion
3 requirements elicitation; interviews and surveys lab 1: requirements doc
4 use case diagrams; actor identification pt 1: use case diagram
5 data flow diagrams; context diagrams; level 0 and 1 dfldasb 2
6 process modeling; decision tables and trees q

2
6 process modeling; decision tables and trees quiz 2
7 data modeling; er diagrams; data dictionary lab 3
8 midterm examination midterm exam
9 system design; input/output design; ui wireframes pt 2: wireframes
10 database design; normalization review lab 4
11 prototyping; low-fidelity and high-fidelity prototypes lab 5
12 system testing; test plans and test cases pt 3: test plan
13 implementation planning; conversion strategies lecture
14 system maintenance; change management quiz 3
15 group p

m maintenance; change management quiz 3
15 group presentations: system design proposal group presentation
16 final examination final exam
4. grading system
component weight
quizzes / participation 15%
laboratory outputs 25%
performance tasks 20%
midterm exam 20%
final exam 20%
5. policies
team work: all group members must contribute equally. division of labor must be documented and
submitted alongside group outputs.
submission: all outputs must be submitted via the lms before 11:59 pm on the due

e submitted via the lms before 11:59 pm on the due date. email
submissions are not accepted.
consultation: students are encouraged to schedule consultations during the instructor's office hours for
project feedback.
6. references
1. kendall, k. kendall, j. (2019). systems analysis and design. pearson.
2. sommerville, i. (2016). software engineering. pearson.
3. dennis, a. et al. (2021). systems analysis and design: an object-oriented approach with uml.
wiley.

h uml.
wiley.

course syllabus: thesis writing and research methods 1st
semester, a.y. 20262027
course information
instructor: research instructor name units: 3 schedule: f 1:004:00 pm room: research lab
1. course description
this course guides fourth-year is students through the research process from topic conceptualization
to final thesis defense. students will develop skills in research design, literature review writing, data
collection methodologies, statistical analysis, and academic writing. by the end o

tical analysis, and academic writing. by the end of the semester,
students will have completed and defended chapters 1 through 3 of their undergraduate thesis.
2. learning outcomes
lo 1: formulate a clear and researchable problem statement relevant to the field of information
systems.
lo 2: conduct a comprehensive review of related literature and synthesize findings.
lo 3: design an appropriate research methodology including data collection and analysis plans.
lo 4: write and defend chapters 1,

nalysis plans.
lo 4: write and defend chapters 1, 2, and 3 of an undergraduate thesis following apa 7th edition
format.
3. chapter and milestone schedule
week milestone output
1 orientation; research ethics; topic brainstormingtopic proposal (1 page)
2 research problem identification; significance of thed rsatfut dcyhapter 1 section 1
3 conceptual framework; theoretical framework framework diagram
4 statement of the problem; research questions; hyppto t1h:e csihsapter 1 draft
5 literature review

yppto t1h:e csihsapter 1 draft
5 literature review strategy; database search annotated bibliography (10 sources)
6 synthesizing related literature literature matrix
7 writing related studies; synthesis paragraph draft chapter 2
8 midterm defense: chapter 1 and chapter 2 midterm defense
9 research design; quantitative vs qualitative methodology draft
10 sampling; population; data collection instrumentssurvey/interview guide
11 validity and reliability; pilot testing instrument validation report
1

lity; pilot testing instrument validation report
12 data collection plan; timeline pt 2: chapter 3 draft
13 statistical tools; data analysis plan analysis framework
14 revision of chapters 13 based on panel feedbacrkevised manuscript
15 pre-final defense rehearsal presentation slides
16 final defense: chapters 1, 2, and 3 final thesis manuscript
4. grading system
component weight
chapter drafts and revisions 20%
annotated bibliography and literature matrix 10%
performance tasks 20%
midterm defen

ure matrix 10%
performance tasks 20%
midterm defense 25%
final defense 25%
5. formatting guidelines
all written outputs must follow apa 7th edition formatting: 12pt times new roman, double-spaced,
1-inch margins, page numbers on upper right.
citations: all sources must be properly cited in-text and listed in the references section. plagiarism will
result in automatic disqualification of the submission.
manuscript submission: final thesis manuscript must be submitted both in soft copy (pdf via lm

pt must be submitted both in soft copy (pdf via lms)
and hard copy (spiral-bound) before the defense date.
6. references and style guide
1. american psychological association. (2020). publication manual of the apa (7th ed.).
2. creswell, j. creswell, j.d. (2018). research design: qualitative, quantitative, and mixed methods
approaches. sage.
3. creswell, j. (2015). educational research: planning, conducting, and evaluating quantitative and
qualitative research. pearson.
ative research. pearson.

attendance policy
a student who incurs absences of more than 20% of the total class hours prescribed for

e than 20% of the total class hours prescribed for the course
shall be dropped from the course and given a grade of dropped (drp).
for a 3-unit subject meeting 3 hours per week (54 hours/semester), the maximum allowable absences
is 10 hours (approximately 34 class sessions depending on schedule).
tardiness: a student who arrives more than 15 minutes after the scheduled start of class will be
marked late. three instances of tardiness are equivalent to one absence.
code of student conduct
students

t to one absence.
code of student conduct
students are expected to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity. cheating, plagiarism,
fabrication of data, and misrepresentation are serious violations subject to disciplinary action.
use of mobile phones during class is prohibited unless explicitly permitted by the instructor for
educational purposes.
students must wear the prescribed school uniform and id at all times while inside the campus.
any form of bullying, harassment, or discrimi

pus.
any form of bullying, harassment, or discrimination against fellow students or faculty members is strictly
prohibited and may result in suspension or expulsion.
scholarship and financial assistance programs
ched scholarship (unifast): available to qualified undergraduate students. applications are
accepted at the beginning of each semester. requirements include a minimum gpa of 2.0 and no
failing grades in the previous semester.
dost-sei scholarship: for science and engineering students. sc

olarship: for science and engineering students. scholars must maintain a qpa of at
least 2.0 and complete 8 hours of community service per semester.
institutional scholarship: students who rank in the top 10% of their year level may qualify for a partial
tuition discount. academic performance is evaluated at the end of each semester.
work-study program: qualified students may apply for the work-study program, which provides a
monthly stipend in exchange for 20 hours of work per week in designate

xchange for 20 hours of work per week in designated campus offices.
library services
the school library is open monday to friday, 7:30 am to 6:00 pm, and saturday, 8:00 am to 12:00 nn.
students must present their school id to borrow materials.
borrowing policy: students may borrow a maximum of 3 books at a time for a period of 3 days.
overdue fines are php 5.00 per day per book.
online resources: students have access to the school's subscribed online databases including
jstor, proquest, and the

line databases including
jstor, proquest, and the philippine elib. login credentials are provided at the start of enrollment.
it and computer laboratory policies
students must log in using their institutional account. unauthorized access to other users' accounts is a
violation of the acceptable use policy.
food and drinks are strictly prohibited inside the computer laboratory. students who damage
equipment due to negligence will be held responsible for repair or replacement costs.
saving of pers

le for repair or replacement costs.
saving of personal files must be done on personal storage devices (usb) or cloud storage. the
laboratory computers are cleared at the end of each semester.

course syllabus: professional ethics in information technology 1st
semester, a.y. 20262027
course information
instructor: ethics instructor name units: 3 schedule: mwf 11:00 am12:00 nn room: room 301
1. course description
this course examines the ethical, legal, and social dimensions of information technology. topics
include professional codes of conduct, intellectual property rights, data privacy and protection laws
(including the philippine data privacy act of 2012), cybercrime legislation (ra

a privacy act of 2012), cybercrime legislation (ra 10175), social media
ethics, algorithmic bias, ai ethics, digital divide, and responsible technology development. students will
analyze real-world case studies and develop frameworks for ethical decision-making in the it
profession.
2. learning outcomes
lo 1: analyze ethical dilemmas in technology using established ethical frameworks such as
utilitarianism, deontology, and virtue ethics.
lo 2: apply philippine laws on data privacy, cybercrime, a

ply philippine laws on data privacy, cybercrime, and intellectual property to real-world it
scenarios.
lo 3: evaluate the social impact of emerging technologies including artificial intelligence, surveillance
systems, and social media platforms.
lo 4: formulate an ethical decision-making framework applicable to professional it practice.
3. weekly schedule
week topic assessment
1 introduction to ethics; it profession and ethical respoqnsuiibzi l1ity
2 ethical frameworks: utilitarianism, deontolog

ty
2 ethical frameworks: utilitarianism, deontology, virtue ceathsiec sstudy 1
3 professional codes of conduct: acm, ieee, dict guiqdeuliizn e2s
4 intellectual property rights; copyright, patents, and lipcetn 1s:e isp i na intalysis paper
5 philippine data privacy act (ra 10173); npc regulaticonasse study 2
6 gdpr overview; cross-border data transfers and coqmupilzia 3nce
7 cybercrime prevention act (ra 10175); legal cases inl apbh: case analysis
8 midterm examination midterm exam
9 social media

8 midterm examination midterm exam
9 social media ethics; fake news; online harassment lpatw s2: ethics position paper
10 algorithmic bias; fairness in ai systems case study 3
11 ai ethics frameworks; unesco recommendation ond aisicussion forum
12 digital divide; accessibility and inclusion in technologpyt 3: policy proposal
13 whistleblowing in it; corporate responsibility quiz 4
14 emerging issues: deepfakes, surveillance capitalism,p baionmel edtriisccsussion
15 student presentations: ethica

edtriisccsussion
15 student presentations: ethical case study analysis group presentation
16 final examination final exam
4. grading system
component weight
quizzes 15%
case studies and discussion 20%
performance tasks 25%
midterm exam 20%
final exam 20%
5. course policies
class discussion: ethical discussions require respect and open-mindedness. students must listen
actively and engage thoughtfully. personal attacks or discriminatory language will not be tolerated.
position papers: must be sub

ill not be tolerated.
position papers: must be submitted in apa format, minimum 3 pages, with at least 5 credible
references. ai-generated papers without proper disclosure and critical analysis will not be accepted.
case studies: group case study presentations must include a clear identification of the ethical issue,
applicable laws, stakeholder analysis, and a recommended course of action.
6. key laws and references
1. republic act 10173 data privacy act of 2012 (philippines).
2. republic act 1

ivacy act of 2012 (philippines).
2. republic act 10175 cybercrime prevention act of 2012 (philippines).
3. republic act 8792 electronic commerce act of 2000 (philippines).
4. unesco recommendation on the ethics of artificial intelligence (2021).
5. acm code of ethics and professional conduct (2018).
6. tavani, h. (2016). ethics and technology: controversies, questions, and strategies for ethical
computing. wiley.

course syllabus: software engineering 1st semester, a.y.
course information
instructor: se instructor name units: 3 schedule: tth 10:30 am12:00 nn room: it lab 1
1. course description
software engineering introduces students to the systematic development of high-quality software. the
course covers software process models, requirements engineering, software design principles, design
patterns, software testing strategies, project management, software quality assurance, and devops
practices. studen

re quality assurance, and devops
practices. students will apply these principles in a group software development project that spans the
entire semester, culminating in a working software prototype and a formal project documentation
package.
2. learning outcomes
lo 1: apply software process models (agile, scrum, waterfall) to plan and manage a software
development project.
lo 2: write clear and testable software requirements using use cases and user stories.
lo 3: apply object-oriented design pri

er stories.
lo 3: apply object-oriented design principles (solid) and common design patterns to software
architecture.
lo 4: design and execute a test plan covering unit, integration, and system testing.
lo 5: use version control (git/github) and ci/cd tools in a team software project.
3. weekly schedule
week topic assessment
1 introduction to software engineering; sdlc models quiz 1
2 agile methodology; scrum framework; sprint planninglab 1: scrum board setup
3 requirements engineering; user st

um board setup
3 requirements engineering; user stories; acceptance claribte 2ri:a requirements doc
4 software architecture; layered, mvc, microservices pt 1: architecture diagram
5 object-oriented design; solid principles quiz 2
6 design patterns: singleton, factory, observer, strateglyab 3: design pattern implementation
7 version control with git; branching strategies; pull relqaube s4t:s github workflow
8 midterm examination midterm exam
9 software testing; unit testing with pytest or junit p

tware testing; unit testing with pytest or junit pt 2: test suite
10 integration testing; system testing; uat lab 5
11 software quality assurance; code reviews; static anaqlyusizis 3
12 devops basics; ci/cd pipelines with github actions pt 3: ci/cd pipeline
13 software project management; estimation; risk managleemctuernet
14 documentation; api documentation; readme best prlaacbti c6es
15 group project presentations: working software demogroup demo
16 final examination final exam
4. grading syst

mo
16 final examination final exam
4. grading system
component weight
quizzes / participation 10%
laboratory exercises 20%
performance tasks 20%
group software project 20%
midterm exam 15%
final exam 15%
5. group project requirements
each group (34 members) must develop a working software application addressing a real-world
problem. the project must use git for version control with a shared repository, maintain a product
backlog, complete at least 3 sprint cycles, and submit a final project docu

t 3 sprint cycles, and submit a final project documentation package including
srs, design documents, test plan, and user manual.
technology stack: students may use any programming language and framework approved by the
instructor. web-based applications (react, django, fastapi) and mobile applications (flutter, react
native) are both acceptable.
6. references
1. sommerville, i. (2016). software engineering (10th ed.). pearson.
2. martin, r.c. (2017). clean architecture. prentice hall.
